coredata.dc:title: Orange carbon dots for Sn (IV) and ascorbic acid sensing in live cells and medicine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Xiao
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: In view of the important influence of Sn4+ and ascorbic acid (AA) on environmental pollution and human health, the establishment of an effective method to achieve rapid and highly sensitive detection is urgently needed. Herein, orange fluorescent (FL) carbon dots (CDs) were successfully prepared from 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride and thiourea via solvothermal approach. CDs can serve as FL sensors for detecting Sn4+ and AA, the detection limit is 0.13 and 0.41 μM, respectively, through a signal on–off–on mode. Furthermore, the practicability of CDs nanoprobes is validated by the successful identification of Sn4+ and AA in live cells, real water samples and medicine. The present work comfirms the feasibility of employing CDs as FL probes to effectively and selectively detect Sn4+ and AA, which presents a potential approach for on-site detection of Sn4+ and AA in various environment and biological systems with exceptional sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Sn (IV)
Ascorbic acid (AA)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Sn4+ and AA by CDs
Detection of Sn4+ and AA in real samples
Sn4+ and AA detection based on CDs
Response mechanism of the CDs probe
Detection of Sn4+ and AA in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: size distribution), (B) XRD pattern, (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (D) XPS full scan and HR XPS of (E) N 1 s and (F) S 2p spectrum of CDs. (G) UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectrum of CDs. Inset: the photographs of CDs solution under natural light (left) and UV light (365 nm, right). (H) FL spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength. (I) FL spectra of CDs at different pH.

(A) Selectivity detection for Sn4+. (B) The FL intensity variation of CDs in response to Sn4+. (C) FL spectra of CDs vs the different concentration of Sn4+. (D) Relationship between the concentration of Sn4+ and FL intensity of CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs with different concentration of Sn4+. (F) The linear relationship between Sn4+ concentration and A480. (G) Selectivity (pink column) and competitive (blue column) detection for AA. (H) The FL intensity variation of CDs-Sn4+ in response to AA. (I) FL spectra of CDs-Sn4+ with different concentration of AA. (J) Relationship between the concentration of AA and FL intensity. (K) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-Sn4+ with different concentration of AA. (L) The linear relationship between AA concentration and A260. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs + Sn4+, CDs + Sn4++AA, AA and Sn4+. (B) Time-resolved FL decay curves of CDs, CDs + Sn4+ and CDs + Sn4++AA. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sn4+ and FL spectrum of CDs. (D) Schematic of the mechanism of CDs sensing Sn4+ and AA.

(A) The FL images of L929 cells treated with CDs and different concentration of Sn4+. (B) Relationship between the concentration of Sn4+ and cellular FL intensity. (C) The FL images of L929 cells treated with CDs + Sn4+ and different concentration AA. (D) Relationship between the concentration of AA and cellular FL intensity.
A diagram illustrating the procedure of CDs synthesis and their applications in Sn4+ and AA detection.
Determination of Sn4+ in actual samples (n = 3).
Determination of AA in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Orange carbon dots for Sn (IV) and ascorbic acid sensing in live cells and medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the important influence of Sn4+ and ascorbic acid (AA) on environmental pollution and human health, the establishment of an effective method to achieve rapid and highly sensitive detection is urgently needed. Herein, orange fluorescent (FL) carbon dots (CDs) were successfully prepared from 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride and thiourea via solvothermal approach. CDs can serve as FL sensors for detecting Sn4+ and AA, the detection limit is 0.13 and 0.41 μM, respectively, through a signal on–off–on mode. Furthermore, the practicability of CDs nanoprobes is validated by the successful identification of Sn4+ and AA in live cells, real water samples and medicine. The present work comfirms the feasibility of employing CDs as FL probes to effectively and selectively detect Sn4+ and AA, which presents a potential approach for on-site detection of Sn4+ and AA in various environment and biological systems with exceptional sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the essential trace elements for humans and animals, tin ion (Sn4+) can facilitate the reaction of protein and nucleic acid in the body, accelerate the body’s growth and impede the proliferation of malignant cells [1–3]. Abnormal levels of Sn4+ in the human body have been found to be closely linked with a range of human diseases. A deficiency in Sn4+ can lead to auditory disorder, impair muscular development and diminish cancer preventive capacity [2,4]. However, excessive intake of Sn4+ will promote hepatic lesions and poisoning, as well as detrimental effects on the nervous system, immune system and chromosomes [5,6]. In contemporary industries, Sn4+ is widely used in the processes of agriculture and industry [6]. Due to its inherent resistance to natural degradation and persistent residue, the significant ecological risks posed by the extensive accumulation of Sn4+ in the environment cannot be overlooked [2].

Ascorbic acid (AA), commonly known as Vitamin C, plays a crucial role in numerous intricate metabolic processes within the body, which enhances the organism’s immunity against infections and exhibits beneficial properties in averting cardiovascular diseases and cancer [7–9]. Given that AA is not naturally produced by the human body, it relies on regular dietary intake or consumption of functional foods [9]. A significant deficiency in AA may result in anemia, impaired fertility and increased cancer incidence [7,10]. Excessive AA intake can cause gastritis, diarrhea, stomach cramps and the formation of kidney stones [7,11]. Hence, it is of utmost importance to devise straightforward, highly responsive and accurate techniques for detecting Sn4+ and AA, which will greatly contribute to environmental management and clinical applications.

At present, a range of techniques are accessible for the identification of Sn4+ and AA content in actual samples, including electrochemical methods [12–14], atomic absorption spectroscopy [15,16], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [17], and high-performance liquid chromatography [18]. Even though these techniques are capable of fulfilling the sensitivity requirements, they are frequently constrained by costly equipment, intricate operational procedures, and the requirement for personnel with advanced expertise. FL analysis [19,20] holds significant potential for sensing due to its easy operation, excellent specificity, remarkable sensitivity and rapid analysis. Several studies on FL detection of Sn4+ or AA have been reported. Yang et al. utilized rhodamine as a FL sensor to recognize Sn4+ within live cells [6]. Ravichandiran et al. prepared acridine derivatives to detect Sn4+ in environmental and biological media [2]. Zhang et al. proposed a technique to assay AA by employing molybdenum disulfide quantum dots [21]. Nevertheless, these probes suffer from the drawbacks of intricate preparation, high detection limits, poor sensitivity and restricted range of applications. Therefore, we believe that developing simple and easily available FL probes for detecting Sn4+ and AA holds immense importance in the domains of ecological environmental management and biological sciences.

FL nanoprobes have garnered significant interest owing to their remarkable FL performance [22]. As a representative, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively employed as FL nanoprobes in analytical methodology. CDs exhibit exceptional optical properties, superior biocompatibility, remarkable spatial structure, adjustable photo-induced luminescence and environmental friendliness [23–26]. These traits accelerate the wide applications of CDs in diverse fields, including biosensing [25,27–30], disease diagnosis [31–35], bioimaging [36–40], information encryption [41–44] and optoelectronics [45,46]. In particular, it is very sensible to design CDs-based FL sensors that specifically recognize target substances.

In this paper, orange CDs were synthesized by solvothermal technique (Scheme 1 ). The FL “on–off–on” model was utilized to identify Sn4+ and AA. The FL of CDs was quenched by Sn4+ and resulting in the FL signal at 557 nm being “off”. Nevertheless, after the addition of AA, a new FL signal at 480 nm was “on”. The limit of detection (LOD) of Sn4+ and AA was 0.13 and 0.41 μM, correspondingly. This technique has been effectively utilized in detecting real water samples and medicine with satisfactory recovery rates. Furthermore, CDs can serve as biosensors for identifying Sn4+ and AA within living cells. Compared with the previous methods, CDs exhibit advantages in terms of simple preparation, exceptional selectivity and rapid response to Sn4+ and AA, thereby enabling their widespread application in the detection of Sn4+ and AA in diverse actual samples. This study holds significant implications for the analysis of environmental pollution and disease research associated with Sn4+ and AA.

1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride (Y15) was acquired from Xiensi Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). SnCl4·5H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Mg(NO3)2, NaCl, KCl, NH4F, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, NaNO2, NiCl2·6H2O, NaAlO2, LiBr, NaBr, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, L-Lysine (L-Lys), cytosine (Cyt), L-Methionine (L-Met), L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp), citric acid, disodium hydrogen phosphate and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was acquired from Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory (Tianjin, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA) was utilized to generate ultrapure water.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was tested on a JEOL JEM-1011 (Japan) with accelerating voltage of 100 kV. The crystallization was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy at Nifiltered Cu Karadiation (l = 1.5406 Å) in the range of 10-80° (Bruker D8). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was measured using a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer over the range of 500–4000 cm−1. X-Ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) data was examined using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique Surface Analysis. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by utilizing a Techcomp UV2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The FL spectra were measured on a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrophotometer. The FL lifetime was obtained by the FLS980 Fluorescence Spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments. The cellular FL images were captured utilizing the Operetta CLSTM High Content Analysis System (PerkinElmer, USA) in the non-confocal mode and 40 × objective lens with a high numerical aperture (NA), which possessed a significantly elevated refractive index.

First, Y15 (30 mg) and thiourea (56 mg) were solubilized in DMF (10 mL), which underwent ultrasonication for 20 min. The obtained solution was subjected to heating in a Teflon reaction vessel with a capacity of 20 mL at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the solution was purified through dialysis (MWCO = 500 Da). Ultimately, a freeze-drying process was employed to acquire CDs in the form of solid powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sn4+ and AA by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of CDs towards Sn4+, 200 μM of solution (Co2+, Li +, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ag+, K+, Sn4+, L-Lys, Cyt, L-Met, L-Asp, AA, Cl-, F-, Br-, SO3 2-, NO2 –, AlO2 -, SO4 2-) was combined with an equal volume of CDs solution (20 μg·mL−1), then, the FL spectra were measured. In order to assess the interference of various substances on Sn4+, a solution was prepared by mixing 30 μg·mL−1 CDs solution (1 mL) with 600 μM Sn4+ solution (1 mL). Subsequently, 1 mL of interfering substances (600 μM) was introduced into the aforementioned mixture. To determine the linear range and LOD of Sn4+, equal volume of CDs solution (20 μg·mL−1) and varying concentration of Sn4+ solution were mixed, followed by recording the FL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. All data were processed through the Origin software. The LOD can be determined by the level of background interference (N) and the sensitivity of the probe (S), using the formula LOD = 3 N/S.

With the aim of determining the linear range and LOD for AA, various concentration of AA solution was added into the CDs-Sn4+ system, the FL and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. To assess the interference of other substances on AA, equal volume of interfering substances (200 μM) was injected into the CDs-Sn4+-AA system under the same experimental condition, and the FL spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sn4+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water plays a crucial role in sustaining human life, serving as a vital resource for daily living and industrial production. Tap water, mineral water, lake water and synthetic urine were chosen as the actual samples. The samples were subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 4000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration through 0.22 µm membrane to remove insoluble impurities. In order to simulate the residue of Sn4+, we added different concentration of Sn4+ into actual samples. By measuring the FL intensity, it is possible to determine the recovery rate of Sn4+ in the actual samples based on the linear equation.

Vitamin C chewable tablets (vitamin C content of each tablet is 0.1 g, Shandong Jiejing Biotechnology Co., Ltd.) and vitamin C effervescent tablets (vitamin C content of each tablet is 0.009 g, Zhejiang Conba Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd.) were purchased from local supermarkets and subsequently pulverized into a fine powder. In order to guarantee the concentration of the sample aqueous solution remained within the detectable range, the solution was diluted to an appropriate level. To maintain authenticity of the sample, any other additional processing was avoided. The solution was mixed with an equivalent volume of CDs-Sn4+ solution and the FL spectra were measured.

Firstly, L929 cells were cultured in a 96-well cell culture cluster with a seeding density of 7 × 103 cells/well. The incubation was carried out at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h to facilitate adherence. Next, a solution comprising 50 μg·mL−1 of CDs was used to replace the culture medium, and the cells were subjected to incubation for different time. After removing the culture medium, L929 cells were washed four times with PBS. Thirdly, 4 % polyformaldehyde solution (100 μL) was introduced to each well, incubated for 10 min to immobilize the cells. Afterwards, the cells were exposed to Hoechst 33,258 solution (100 μL) for 5 min away from light, and subsequently cell washing was performed. Finally, the cell culture clusters were placed in the High Content Analysis System to observe cell morphology and FL (λex: 435 ∼ 460 nm, λem: 570 ∼ 650 nm). The FL intensity of cells was quantified by ImageJ software.

Detecting Sn4+ in live cells. The cells were exposed to CDs solution for a duration of 6 h, the initial culture medium was substituted with a culture medium containing varying concentration of Sn4+ for an additional hour. The subsequent steps remained consistent with the above instructions (λex: 435 ∼ 460 nm, λem: 570 ∼ 650 nm).

Detecting AA in live cells. The cells were exposed with CDs solution for 6 h and then exposed to Sn4+ culture medium (200 μM) for 1 h. The AA culture medium with varying concentration was subsequently introduced as the new culture medium, followed by an additional incubation for 1 h. The subsequent steps were consistent with the aforementioned procedure (λex: 435 ∼ 460 nm, λem: 470 ∼ 550 nm).

The TEM technique was employed to analyze the morphology of CDs. The TEM image reveals that CDs exhibit a distinctive spherical morphology and excellent dispersion (Fig. 1 A). The size of CDs particles varies between 4.06 and 8.98 nm, with an average diameter of 6.26 ± 0.93 nm. Additionally, XRD pattern was utilized to analyze the crystallinity of CDs. There is a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.7°, which can be attributed to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, unequivocally suggesting the presence of a graphitic structure in CDs (Fig. 1B). Afterwards, the FT-IR spectroscopy technique was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of CDs (Fig. 1C). The peaks at 2059 and 1015 cm−1 are attributed to N = C = S and C–S, which reveal the doping of nitrogen and sulfur in CDs [47]. The peaks at 3155–3460 cm−1 origins from the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H, the hydrophilic groups endow CDs with excellent water solubility [38]. The presence of C–H is observed at 2938 and 2778 cm−1. The peaks at 1628, 1500, 1381, and 1271 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C = C, C–N, and C–O bonds respectively [38]. Furthermore, the elemental composition and surface functional groups of CDs were also analyzed using XPS. In the XPS full-scan spectrum, there are four typical peaks at 162.0, 285.0, 400.0, and 532.9 eV, demonstrating CDs consist of the elements S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, and the elemental content is 1.09 %, 76.75 %, 8.42 %, and 13.74 %, respectively (Fig. 1D). There are three peaks in the C 1 s high-resolution (HR) XPS spectrum (Fig. S1A), which can be ascribed to C = O/C = N (288.8 eV), C–N/C–S/C–O (285.7 eV) and C = C/C–C (284.8 eV) [48,49]. The N 1 s HR XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows two distinct peaks, representing amino C–N (400.0 eV) and C = N (398.8 eV) [50]. There are two peaks in the O 1 s HR XPS spectrum (Fig. S1B), which originates from C–OH (533.4 eV) and C = O (532.2 eV) [49]. The two peaks in the HR XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1F) are attributed to S 2p1/2 (163.3 eV) and S 2p3/2 (162.0 eV) [48].

The CDs solution exhibits a pale pink under natural light, while it emits orange FL upon exposure to UV light (365 nm, photos inserted in Fig. 1G). The absorption peak at 264 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of CDs primarily arises from the π-π* transition of the C = C in the aromatic chromophore structure (Fig. 1G). Additionally, the presence of a broad absorption band at 400–575 nm is due to the n-π* transition associated with the C = O/C = N. In addition, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength is 400 and 557 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength varies from 400 to 470 nm, although the FL intensity of CDs changes, the emission wavelength is almost constant, demonstrating the FL is independent of excitation (Fig. 1H). CDs exhibit variations in FL intensity when subjected to different pH conditions (Fig. 1I). However, the FL intensity is not sensitive when operating within a near-neutral range. The FL intensity reaches its maximum at pH = 7.0. Thus, all the following experiments were carried out at pH = 7.0.

Superior probes require excellent FL stability across diverse environments. The adaptability of CDs in complex and ever-changing environments of organisms was evaluated. It was observed that at a concentration of 400 μM NaCl, the FL intensity of CDs presented 1 % attenuation, indicating the strong ability to resist ion interference (Fig. S2A). In addition, even after being exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp for a duration of 10 h, the CDs solution still maintains excellent FL intensity, demonstrating the remarkable resistance against photobleaching (Fig. S2B). It is surprising that the FL attenuation does not exceed 2 % after storage for 30 days, which is essential in terms of long-term tracking and detection (Fig. S2C). Given the exceptional stability, CDs hold immense promise for utilization in the realm of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sn4+ and AA detection based on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As an effective FL probe, it is crucial for CDs to exhibit prompt and precise responsiveness towards Sn4+, thereby enhancing their potential utility in Sn4+ detection. When various substances were introduced into the CDs solution, it was observed that only Sn4+ reduced the FL of CDs obviously, which demonstrates the remarkable specificity of CDs toward Sn4+ (Fig. 2 A). In addition, CDs exhibits rapid response within one minute (Fig. 2B). With the rise of Sn4+ concentration, there is a gradual decline in the FL intensity exhibited by CDs (Fig. 2C). When the Sn4+ concentration reaches 200 μM, a more than 89 % FL attenuation is observed, indicating that Sn4+ can cause the FL signal of CDs in an “off” state. The Stern-Volmer curve depicts the FL intensity of CDs vs Sn4+ concentration (Fig. 2D). In the range of 0–10 μM, F0/F − 1 = 0.0668 [Sn4+] + 0.0061, R2 = 0.9887, and the LOD was 0.13 μM. In the range of 10–100 μM, F0/F − 1 = 0.0237[Sn4+] + 0.3856, R2 = 0.9975, the LOD was 0.37 μM. F0 and F represent the FL intensity of CDs when Sn4+ is absent and present, and [Sn4+] refers to the concentration of Sn4+. The UV–vis absorption spectra reveal the absorbance at 480 nm, exhibit a linear response within the range of 0–10 μM and the linear equation is A480/A0 = 0.0195[Sn4+] + 1.0202 (R2 = 0.9922) (Fig. 2E–F), the LOD is 0.092 μM. A0 and A480 represent the absorbance of CDs in the absence and presence of Sn4+ at 480 nm. The result suggests that CDs exhibit remarkable sensing abilities toward Sn4+, which holds great potential in identifying trace amount of Sn4+ within samples obtained from the environment.

Afterwards, the response ability of CDs-Sn4+ probe towards AA was evaluated. Upon introducing various substances into the CDs-Sn4+ system, a noticeable enhancement in FL intensity was observed upon the addition of AA (Fig. 2G). When AA was introduced into the CDs-Sn4+ system, the FL intensity stabilized at 4 min (Fig. 2H). Introduction of interfering substances into the CDs-Sn4+-AA system did not result in significant changes in FL intensity, verifying the excellent anti-interference ability of CDs-Sn4+(Fig. 2G). However, compared with that of CDs, the emission peak of CDs-Sn4+ underwent blue shift to 480 nm and exhibited a gradual FL enhancement with increasing the concentration of AA (Fig. 2I). Thus, the Sn4+ induced quenching of CDs in the “off” state can be reversed to an “on” state by AA. The CDs-Sn4+ probe exhibits good response towards AA (Fig. 2J). In the intervals from 0 to 70 μM, the linear equation is F/F0 − 1 = 0.0267[AA] − 0.0118 and the LOD is 0.41 μM (R2 = 0.9984). Within the scope of 70–300 μM, the equation is F/F0 − 1 = 0.0104[AA] + 1.1572 and the LOD is 1.04 μM (R2 = 0.9997). F0 and F represent the FL intensity of CDs-Sn4+ in the absence and presence of AA, and [AA] refers to the concentration of AA. Likewise, variation in the absorbance at 260 nm can be found in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2K), and a good linear relationship was obtained within the scope of 0–70 μM and the linear equation is A260/A0 = 0.0067[AA] + 0.9657, with LOD = 0.23 μM (R2 = 0.9908) (Fig. 2L). A260 and A0 represent the absorbance of CDs-Sn4+ when AA is present and absent. The distinguished sensing ability of CDs-Sn4+ probe for AA makes it promising for sensing AA with high selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of the CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of CDs selectively recognizing Sn4+ and AA was investigated. Upon the addition of Sn4+ to CDs, the absorbance in the range of 400–600 nm exhibits a strengthening trend (Fig. 3 A). Moreover, FL lifetime increases from 3.76 to 4.13 ns (Fig. 3B). The results suggest the presence of a dynamic quenching mechanism. In addition, no apparent overlap can be observed between the FL spectra of CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of Sn4+ (Fig. 3C). Thus, the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer is ruled out. Based on hard-soft acid-base theory, the –NH and –C = O groups present in CDs can be classified as soft marginal bases, the Sn4+ is considered a soft marginal acid. Hence, it is highly likely that coordination will take place between Sn4+ and the surface –NH and –C = O groups of CDs, resulting in FL attenuation [2]. In addition, the presence of Sn4+ facilitates the intramolecular electron transfer (ICT) from the excited state of CDs to the unoccupied d orbitals of Sn4+, resulting in the quenching of FL (Fig. 3D) [2]. However, the presence of AA causes a reduction from Sn4+ to Sn2+, which leads to the elimination of the ICT effect, thereby restoring the FL of CDs. Therefore, the CDs-Sn4+ system exhibits potential as an “on” FL sensor for detecting AA.

To expand the biological applications of CDs, it is crucial to consider the labeling of Sn4+ and AA in live cells. Therefore, the toxicity and bioimaging capability of CDs were investigated. In the MTT experiments, we chose L929 and HeLa cells as representatives. When the level of CDs reaches a concentration of 100 μg·mL−1, cellular viability remains consistently above 95 %, suggesting that CDs possess excellent cytocompatibility (Fig. S3A). L929 cells were treated with CDs for various time intervals, and then observed by High Content Analysis System. As depicted in Fig. S3B, the FL emitted by the cells is proportional to the incubation time, proving that the uptake of CDs by the cells is time-dependent. The maximum FL was achieved after 6 h of incubation (Fig. S3C).

After exposing cells to CDs for a duration of 6 h, different concentration of Sn4+ was added. As presented in Fig. 4 A, with the progressive rise in Sn4+ concentration, the FL showed a decreasing trend. After that, the FL intensity was quantified and a good linear correlation was observed between the concentration of Sn4+ and the FL intensity (Fig. 4B). The linear equation is I = − 0.6907[Sn4+] + 96.8644 (R2 = 0.9870), where I represents the FL intensity of cells. After incubation with CDs and Sn4+, the cells underwent treatment with varying concentration of AA. As the AA concentration gradually increased, the FL intensity was heightened (Fig. 4C). After quantifying the FL intensity, the linear equation was obtained, I = 0.1446[AA] + 8.9855 (R2 = 0.9922) (Fig. 4D). The findings suggest that CDs can effectively detect Sn4+ and AA in live cells through an “on–off–on” FL switch.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sn4+ and AA in actual samples

ce:section.ce:para: In brief, a simple solvothermal method was utilized to produce water-soluble CDs with orange FL emission, which have unique photostability and outstanding biocompatibility. The FL of CDs is effectually quenched by Sn4+, however, it can be apparently recovered upon the addition of AA. Therefore, a rapid and highly selective FL assay was devised for the detection of Sn4+ and AA using an “on–off–on” approach, the LOD is 0.13 μM (linear range: 0–10 μM) and 0.41 μM (linear range: 0–70 μM), respectively. Moreover, the CDs-based nanosensors were successfully applied for determining Sn4+ and AA in living cells, actual water samples and medicine with promising results. This study showcases the promising utilization of CDs as exceptionally effective optical detectors for biological and environmental monitoring applications.
Xiao Jin: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110784.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biological activity of organotin compounds-an overview
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.J. Snoeij, A.H. Penninks, W. Seinen, Biological activity of organotin compounds-an overview, Environ. Res. 44 (1987) 335–353, https://doi.org/10.1016/s0013-9351(87)80242-6.
